author: potuz
content: <@269786774660710400> Perhaps you guys have thought of this problem and discarded it. Can you explain me if this is proven to not exist? I see that there's a lot of effort in getting signature aggregation for hash based signatures, and that the aggregator would have to produce  a ZK proof. But I would have expected that the natural way to solve for attestations would be to keep using BLS keys in the hot path of attesting/aggregating, just that these BLS keys are just temporary and they are periodically being updated by a PQ secure signature that the validators produce outside of the hot path. As long as this periodicity is longer than the time it takes for a quantum computer to break a considerable amount of the stake, we should keep forkchoice safety.

author: justindrake
content:
Ephemeral keys are being studied as a potential temporary solution for the EL, e.g. https://ethresear.ch/t/achieving-quantum-safety-through-ephemeral-key-pairs-and-account-abstraction/24273 I also think ephemeral keys may work as a temporary solution for the p2p layer.

Having said that, for attestations, ephemeral keys may interfere with the slashing conditions where the slot number(s) of the slashable attestation(s) could be significantly smaller than the current slot number. Ephemeral keys are also on somewhat shaky foundations because quantum computers may eventually run faster than we expect.

author: potuz
content: hmm, yeah it's similar to ephemereal keys. I can see the point in slashings but that also doesn't sound like a fundamental problem. For example for surround votes, the commitment to both keys is on chain and you can validate both slashings belong to the same validator.  For double voting the ephemereal key could not have changed in the two attestations.

author: potuz
content: So only for surround votes on completeley separate chains is that this may be a problem, but it sounds like something that can be easily solved.

author: justindrake
content: Another issue with temporary BLS keys is re-registration. Having to reregister keys every few minutes would be expensive. (I don't see how to carry over aggregation in the context of re-registration.)

author: potuz
content: oh wait you think the time for getting say 10% of the stake in Ethereum would be around 10'? If that' s the case then yeah, you can forget about this idea. I was thinking that with hours/days we would be fine for years. In this case then those registrations can be settled in blobs safely
